steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: The blank joins two independent clauses: "The book wasn't written solely by Kittredge" and "other authors, such as Linda Hogan and Barry Lopez, also contributed essays." The contrast belongs with the first clause's sole-authorship claim, and the second clause supports that contrast by naming other contributors.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Kittredge, however;’: The comma binds "however" to the first clause, qualifying the claim that the book was written solely by Kittredge. The semicolon then joins the second independent clause about the other contributors. This places the contrast exactly where the sentence needs it. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘Kittredge; however,’: This makes "however" introduce the second clause, so the other authors' contributions would be framed as contrasting with the idea that the book was not written solely by Kittredge. Those ideas actually support each other, so the logic is wrong. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Kittredge. However,’: Like choice B, this makes the second clause carry the contrast, even though it supports the first clause rather than contrasting with it. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Kittredge, however,’: Comma before and comma after "however" joins two independent clauses with only commas — a comma splice. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
